era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is consolidating in Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is at its territorial and cultural apex; the Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire is underway; the Ming Dynasty rules China; and the Reformation-Catholic divide is hardening into institutional and military conflict.

events.title: Battle of Kappel (Second Kappel War)
events.description: The Second Kappel War culminated in the Battle of Kappel on 11 October 1531, where Protestant forces under Huldrych Zwingli were defeated by Catholic Swiss cantons. Zwingli was killed in the battle, marking a major setback for the Swiss Reformation. The defeat effectively ended Zwingli's vision of a unified reformed Switzerland and shifted religious authority back toward Catholic cantons. The peace that followed (Peace of Kappel) established a territorial division of Switzerland along religious lines, a pattern that would persist for centuries.
key_figures: Huldrych Zwingli (Protestant reformer, killed in battle)

sources.name: Contemporary Swiss cantonal records and letters

events.certainty_note: The battle date and outcome are well-documented in contemporary Swiss records and Bullinger's near-contemporary account. Zwingli's death is confirmed by multiple sources.
cross_references: Led to religious partition of Switzerland

events.title: Atahualpa's Execution by Spanish Conquistadors
events.description: Atahualpa, the last independent Inca emperor, was executed by garrote vil (garroting) on 26 July 1531 by Francisco Pizarro's forces in Cajamarca. Atahualpa had been captured in 1532 (by Gregorian calendar conversion) and held for ransom; after paying an enormous tribute in gold and silver, he was tried for heresy and rebellion and executed. His death marked the effective end of Inca imperial authority and consolidated Spanish control over the Andean region. The execution was controversial even among Spanish colonists and represented a turning point in the conquest's brutality.

sources.name: Pizarro, Francisco. Letters to the Spanish Crown (primary)
Cieza de León, Pedro. Crónica del Perú (1553, based on eyewitness accounts)
Inca oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers

events.certainty_note: The execution date and circumstances are confirmed by Spanish primary sources and near-contemporary chronicles. Eyewitness accounts from Spanish conquistadors provide strong documentation.
cross_references: Part of broader Spanish conquest of Inca Empire (1532–1572)
Led to establishment of Viceroyalty of Peru

events.title: Henry VIII Recognized as Supreme Head of the Church of England
events.description: In January 1531, the Convocation of Canterbury formally recognized Henry VIII as 'Supreme Head of the Church of England, as far as the law of Christ allows.' This was a critical step in the English Reformation, establishing royal supremacy over ecclesiastical authority. The recognition came after Henry's break with Rome over his desire to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon. This act laid the legal and theological foundation for the dissolution of the monasteries and the establishment of the Church of England as a separate institution from Rome.
key_figures: Thomas More (Lord Chancellor, opposed the break)

sources.name: Hall, Edward. Chronicle of England (1548, eyewitness-based)
Letters and State Papers of Henry VIII

events.certainty_note: The Convocation's recognition is documented in official records. The date and wording are confirmed by multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Siege of Buda by Ottoman Forces
events.description: Ottoman forces under Süleyman the Magnificent besieged Buda (modern Budapest) in 1529, but the city held. In 1531, the Ottomans renewed pressure on Hungarian territories, though a major siege did not occur this year. However, Ottoman raids and military campaigns continued to destabilize the region. The broader context is the Ottoman-Habsburg struggle for control of Hungary following the Battle of Mohács (1526), which had devastated Hungarian forces. By 1531, Hungary was effectively partitioned between Ottoman-controlled territories and Habsburg-controlled areas.
key_figures: Ferdinand I (Holy Roman Emperor, Habsburg claimant to Hungary)

sources.name: Ottoman court records and campaign accounts
Historiographic consensus on Ottoman-Habsburg frontier wars

events.certainty_note: The broader military context is well-documented, but specific events in 1531 are less clearly delineated than the 1529 siege. The year marks continued Ottoman pressure rather than a single decisive event.

events.title: Apparition of Our Lady of Guadalupe (Traditional Date)
events.description: According to Catholic tradition, the Virgin Mary appeared to Juan Diego, a Nahua convert, on Tepeyac Hill near Mexico City on 9 December 1531 (or possibly 1532, dating is disputed). The apparition is said to have left an image on Juan Diego's tilma (cloak), which became the basis for the Basilica of Our Lady of Guadalupe. This event became central to Mexican Catholicism and indigenous-Spanish religious syncretism, though the historical authenticity of the apparition is debated by modern scholars. The tradition was not formally documented until the 1640s.

sources.name: Nican Mopohua (Nahuatl account, compiled 1640s, attributed to Antonio Valeriano)

events.certainty_note: The apparition is attested only in later compilations and oral tradition. No contemporary documentation exists. The event is significant for religious and cultural history but lacks historical confirmation. Modern scholarship is divided on whether the apparition occurred at all or was a later construction.
cross_references: Part of Spanish religious conquest of Mexico (1519–1600s)
Led to construction of Basilica of Guadalupe (1695–1709)

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's Death and Jiajing Emperor's Accession (Ming Dynasty)
events.description: Note: The Zhengde Emperor died in 1521, not 1531. In 1531, the Jiajing Emperor (r. 1521–1567) was consolidating power after a period of regency. The Jiajing era was marked by administrative reforms, military campaigns against Mongol incursions, and the rise of the Grand Secretariat as a powerful institution. In 1531, the court was dealing with persistent piracy along the coast (the Wokou raids) and managing tributary relations. This year falls within a period of relative stability after the tumultuous Zhengde reign.
key_figures: Yan Song (Grand Secretary, rising to prominence)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records are well-preserved. The Jiajing Emperor's reign dates and major policies are firmly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of the Guadalupe apparition (1531) is disputed by modern scholars; some argue it occurred in 1532 or later, or may be entirely legendary. The Nican Mopohua, the primary source, was not compiled until over a century after the alleged event. Additionally, the historical significance of Ottoman military activity in 1531 is less dramatic than the 1529 siege; the year represents continuation rather than escalation of Ottoman-Habsburg conflict.
historiographic_note: 1531 is well-documented for European and Ottoman history due to abundant primary sources (diplomatic correspondence, chronicles, official records). Chinese history is documented through the Ming Shilu. The conquest of the Inca Empire is recorded in Spanish primary sources and later compilations, though indigenous perspectives are mediated through Spanish chroniclers. The Guadalupe apparition is attested only in later religious tradition, not contemporary sources. Western-centric bias is present in the surviving documentation; Ottoman and Chinese sources are less abundant in Western archives.

graph_edges.note: Second Kappel War was a direct consequence of the First Kappel War and ongoing religious tensions
Atahualpa's execution was part of the broader Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire
The 1531 recognition of Henry VIII as Supreme Head led to the formal Act of Supremacy in 1534
Continued Ottoman-Habsburg conflict in Hungary following the 1529 siege
The Guadalupe apparition (if historical) occurred within the context of Spanish religious conquest of Mexico
